title: The Saturday repair café in our town
summary: Describe a place where people fix broken things together instead of throwing them away, and why it matters.
topic: A place where people fix broken things together
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences describing a place where people repair broken things together, such as a repair café or a workshop. Describe what happens there, who helps, and why it is useful. Use present simple, present perfect, and the connectors 'instead of' and 'so'.
hintStarters: On the first Saturday of every month, the old library hall becomes…
People bring broken toasters, torn coats, and…
Instead of buying a new one, you can…
I went there with a broken lamp, and what surprised me most was…
sampleText: On the first Saturday of every month, the old library hall in my town in Ghent becomes a repair café. People carry in broken things: toasters, lamps, radios, torn coats, and once a wooden chair with three legs. At each table there is a volunteer who knows how to fix one kind of thing. One man only repairs small electric machines. A woman near the window sews and patches clothes. They do the work for free, but they ask you to sit and watch, so you learn a little while they fix your thing. Instead of buying a new toaster, you bring the old one and you find out it only needed a new wire. I have been three times now. The first time, I brought a lamp that had stopped working, and the volunteer showed me the loose part inside in two minutes. He said most broken things are not really broken; they only need one small repair. The café has saved a lot of objects from the bin, and it has saved people money too. But the best part is not the money. It is sitting next to a stranger who teaches you how to keep something alive a little longer. I think every town should have one.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for experience up to now
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'I have been three times now' và 'The café has saved a lot of objects' dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành để nói về kinh nghiệm hoặc kết quả tính đến hiện tại, không nói rõ thời điểm cụ thể. Công thức là 'have/has + quá khứ phân từ'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The sentences 'I have been three times now' and 'The café has saved a lot of objects' use the present perfect to talk about experience or results up to now, without naming an exact time. The pattern is 'have/has + past participle'.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'Instead of' + -ing for an alternative
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Instead of buying a new toaster, you bring the old one' dùng 'instead of' theo sau là động từ thêm -ing để nói về một lựa chọn thay thế cho việc khác.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The sentence 'Instead of buying a new toaster, you bring the old one' uses 'instead of' followed by a verb with -ing to talk about one choice as an alternative to another.

sampleTranslationVi: Vào thứ Bảy đầu tiên mỗi tháng, hội trường thư viện cũ ở thị trấn của tôi tại Ghent trở thành một quán sửa đồ. Người ta mang đến những món hỏng: máy nướng, đèn, radio, áo khoác rách, và có lần cả một chiếc ghế gỗ ba chân. Ở mỗi bàn có một tình nguyện viên biết cách sửa một loại đồ. Một người đàn ông chỉ sửa máy điện nhỏ. Một phụ nữ gần cửa sổ may và vá quần áo. Họ làm việc miễn phí, nhưng họ yêu cầu bạn ngồi xem, để bạn học được một chút trong khi họ sửa đồ cho bạn. Thay vì mua một chiếc máy nướng mới, bạn mang chiếc cũ đến và phát hiện ra nó chỉ cần một sợi dây mới. Đến nay tôi đã đến ba lần. Lần đầu, tôi mang một chiếc đèn đã ngừng hoạt động, và tình nguyện viên chỉ cho tôi bộ phận lỏng bên trong trong hai phút. Anh ấy nói hầu hết đồ hỏng không thật sự hỏng; chúng chỉ cần một lần sửa nhỏ. Quán đã cứu nhiều món đồ khỏi thùng rác, và cũng giúp người ta tiết kiệm tiền. Nhưng điều hay nhất không phải là tiền. Đó là việc ngồi cạnh một người lạ dạy bạn cách giữ một món đồ dùng được lâu hơn một chút. Tôi nghĩ mọi thị trấn nên có một quán như vậy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best-written sentence about the hall changing on Saturdays.
options: On the first Saturday of every month, the hall become a repair café.
In the first Saturday of every month, the hall becomes a repair café.
On the first Saturday of every month, the hall is becoming a repair café.
On the first Saturday of every month, the hall becomes a repair café.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present simple 'becomes' fits a regular event. 'Become' drops the 's'; 'in the first Saturday' uses the wrong preposition; the continuous is unnatural for a routine.
mcQuestions.hint: Which preposition do we use with a specific day: 'on' or 'in'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the most natural sentence using 'instead of'.
options: Instead of buying a new toaster, you bring the old one.
Instead of buy a new toaster, you bring the old one.
Instead of to buy a new toaster, you bring the old one.
Instead buying a new toaster, you bring the old one.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Instead of + -ing' is correct. 'Instead of buy' and 'instead of to buy' use the wrong form; 'instead buying' drops 'of'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'instead of', what form of the verb do we use?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct present perfect sentence about visiting.
options: I have been three times ago.
I have been three times now.
I have went three times now.
I am been three times now.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have been' is correct present perfect for experience. 'Ago' needs the past simple; 'have went' uses the wrong participle; 'am been' is ungrammatical.
mcQuestions.hint: Does 'ago' go with present perfect or past simple?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the best sentence about the volunteer's skill.
options: At each table there is a volunteer who knows how fix things.
At each table there is a volunteer who know how to fix things.
At each table there is a volunteer who knows how to fix things.
At each table there are a volunteer who knows how to fix things.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Knows how to fix' is correct. Option 2 drops 'to'; 'who know' lacks the third-person 's'; 'there are a volunteer' has subject-verb disagreement.
mcQuestions.hint: Is it 'know how to fix' or 'know how fix'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural sentence about doing the work without charge.
options: They do the work for the free.
They make the work for free.
They do the work freely.
They do the work for free.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'For free' is the natural phrase. 'For the free' adds a wrong article; 'make the work' is the wrong verb; 'freely' changes the meaning.
mcQuestions.hint: Which is the fixed phrase meaning 'without payment'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the best sentence about a lamp that stopped working.
options: I brought a lamp that had stopped working.
I brought a lamp that had stop working.
I bringed a lamp that had stopped working.
I brought a lamp who had stopped working.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Had stopped working' and 'brought' are correct. 'Had stop' uses the wrong form; 'bringed' is wrong; 'who' should be 'that' for an object.
mcQuestions.hint: Do we use 'who' or 'that' for a thing like a lamp?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural sentence about most broken things.
options: The most broken things are not really broken.
Most broken things are not really broken.
Most of broken things are not really broken.
Most broken things is not really broken.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Most broken things' is correct for a general statement. 'The most' changes the meaning; 'most of' needs 'the'; 'things is' has subject-verb disagreement.
mcQuestions.hint: For a general group, do we say 'most things' or 'the most things'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the best sentence about saving objects.
options: The café has saved a lot of objects from the bin already since.
The café have saved a lot of objects from the bin.
The café has saved a lot of objects from the bin.
The café has save a lot of objects from the bin.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Has saved' is correct present perfect. Option 2 adds a confusing 'already since'; 'have saved' should be 'has'; 'has save' uses the wrong form.
mcQuestions.hint: With 'the café' as subject, is it 'has' or 'have'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural sentence about what the volunteer said.
options: He said most broken things only needs one small repair.
He said that most broken things only need one small repairs.
He said most broken things only need a one small repair.
He said most broken things only need one small repair.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Things … need one small repair' agrees and is singular for the count noun. Option 2 adds a wrong 's' to 'need'; option 3 makes 'repair' plural after 'one'; option 4 adds a wrong article.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'one', is the noun singular or plural?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the best closing sentence about every town having one.
options: I think every town should have one.
I think every town should has one.
I think every towns should have one.
I think every town should to have one.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Should have' is correct after the modal. 'Should has' is wrong after a modal; 'every towns' should be singular; 'should to have' adds a wrong 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'should', do we use 'have', 'has', or 'to have'?

criticalThinkingQuestion: We often find it easier to buy something new than to repair what we own. What do we lose, besides money, when we stop knowing how to fix our own things?
